turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 17-1201, Thacker v. Tennessee Valley Authority.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: The Tennessee Valley Authority or TVA for short is a government owned corporation that supplies electric power to millions of Americans. It got its start in the Great Depression as an agency tasked with promoting the Tennessee Valley's economic development. But over the decades as its initial projects were completed, the TVA has come more and more to resemble the many privately owned utility companies like Con Ed or Dominion that generate and distribute electricity. This case asks whether the TVA can be sued just as those private companies can or instead whether the TVA's public ownership protects it from legal challenges. The case arises from a boating accident on the Tennessee River. TVA employees were attempting to replace a power line crossing that river. When a cable failed, the power line fell into the water, and just as the workers were raising the line, Gary Thacker drove his boat into the area at high speed. The boat and line collided, seriously injuring Thacker and killing a passenger. Thacker sued the TVA for negligence. He said the TVA had failed to exercise reasonable care in handling power lines and warning boaters of hazardous conditions. The problem is that the TVA is part of the federal government and the federal government has what is called sovereign immunity from lawsuits, unless Congress has waived that immunity. The TVA argued in this case that sovereign immunity protected it from Thacker suit even if it raised a meritorious claim. Now, in general government agencies have a good deal of immunity from tort actions like this one. Congress has waived immunity from some tort suits, but that waiver does not apply to any suit challenging what is called a discretionary function, an activity that involves some choice or discretion on the part of government employees. The Courts below held that the same rule should apply to litigation against the TVA and as a result Thacker's suit should be dismissed. We disagree, because we do not think the same rule applies to the TVA. When Congress created the TVA, it provided that the TVA could sue and be sued in its corporate name. That quite broad waiver of immunity reflects a judgment that the TVA is different from most government agencies, because it is engaging in commercial activities no different from those of private corporations. And when it does so Congress thought, it should be subject to suit in the same way as those private businesses. They do not have so-called discretionary function immunity and neither should the TVA. But that is not quite the end of the matter, because even as the TVA performs many commercial activities, it also engages in some governmental ones. For example, the TVA has law enforcement responsibilities. It may investigate crimes and arrest wrongdoers, and similarly the TVA has the power of eminent domain, which means that like the government, but not like any private actor, it can take people's property for public purposes. And when a suit challenges how the TVA performs those and any other governmental activities, we conclude that Congress meant to provide some measure of immunity. As we held in another case, a Court should recognize from suits of that kind when it is clearly shown to be necessary to avoid grave interference with the performance of a governmental function. So we remand this case for the Courts below to decide whether Thacker's suit challenges governmental or commercial conduct. If governmental, the TVA may have immunity. But if commercial, the kind of thing a private company might also do, then there is no immunity and Thacker's suit may proceed. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
